title: Which countries include mumps vaccines in their national vaccination programs?
subtitle: This shows which countries provide and recommend mumps vaccines through routine services. People may still be able to receive the vaccine if it's not in the routine schedule – it might be optional or available commercially.
caption:
Which countries include mumps vaccines in their national vaccination programs? 2024
This shows which countries provide and recommend mumps vaccines through routine services. People may still be able to receive the vaccine if it's not in the routine schedule – it might be optional or available commercially.

The choropleth map displays the global status of mumps vaccine inclusion in national routine immunization schedules for the year 2024. Countries are shaded to indicate whether the vaccine is administered across the entire country, not routinely administered, or administered only in specific regions. The map reveals a distinct geographic divide in vaccine policy. The "Entire country" category, shown in teal, covers the vast majority of the Americas, including the United States, Canada, Brazil, and Argentina. This status also applies to nearly all of Europe, including the United Kingdom, France, Germany, and Spain, as well as Australia, New Zealand, and Japan. In contrast, the "Not routinely administered" category, colored in reddish-orange, dominates the African continent, encompassing nations such as Nigeria, Ethiopia, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo. This category also includes most of South Asia, including India and Pakistan, as well as Indonesia and several countries in the Middle East like Saudi Arabia and Yemen. A small number of countries, including Morocco and Algeria, fall into the "Regions of the country" category, represented by a tan color, indicating partial implementation. Greenland is marked with diagonal hatching to signify that no data is available. The visual pattern shows that routine, nationwide mumps vaccination is standard practice in high-income regions of the Northern Hemisphere and Oceania, while it is largely absent from national programs across Africa and South Asia.
